idea: By August 2025, DeepMind will release a blog post or paper retracting a previous claim about AI achieving 'silver-medal level' at the International Mathematical Olympiad, after external researchers find that the system was given hints in the problem statements.

By August 2025, at least one major professional sports league (NBA, NFL, MLB, NHL) will publicly ban the use of AI-driven player injury prediction models by team medical staff, citing reliability concerns and player privacy.
By mid-2025, a major cryptocurrency exchange will halt trading of a stablecoin after an AI model designed for fraud detection triggers a false positive that causes a bank run.

A major open-source LLM will be 'killed' not by a lawsuit but by a single critical bug that makes it indistinguishable from a random text generator for a specific language (like Bengali, Swahili, or Tagalog) due to data-sparsity errors in the tokenizer, and this will go unnoticed for months.

A major tech company will be forced to recall an AI-powered hardware product (like a smart speaker or robot) because the AI's voice recognition will systematically mishear a common word in a specific regional dialect, causing the device to perform dangerous actions (e.g., turning off a stove instead of a light).

By December 2025, a leading AI coding assistant (GitHub Copilot or Cursor) will introduce a feature that automatically inserts subtle, non-obvious bugs into generated code to force mandatory human review before production deployment, following a publicized security incident.
The average cost to run a single query on a state-of-the-art LLM (like GPT-5 class) will be higher in 12 months than today, due to increased model size and scarcity of high-end GPUs.
By Q4 2025, the number of academic papers claiming 'human-level performance' on a benchmark will drop by at least 30% compared to Q4 2024.
At least one US state will pass a law requiring AI-generated code to have a mandatory comment header marking it as AI-generated, similar to existing AI image labeling laws.

By December 2025, the single most common runtime error in production AI systems will be a silent float overflow in the temperature parameter of a language model's text generation, causing the model to output pure garbage without any error log, and this will be the root cause of at least one service outage at a unicorn startup.
By Q4 2025, at least one major US hospital system will publicly announce that it has discontinued use of a commercial AI diagnostic tool due to poor performance on diverse patient populations.

A large telecom company will publicly replace its AI-powered customer support chatbot with a simple decision tree, issuing a press release titled 'Simplicity is the New AI' after a study shows customers prefer the faster, error-free non‑AI system.

In the next 12 months, the number of AI-generated Wikipedia articles that are deleted for lack of notability will exceed the number of human-written articles deleted for the same reason, as measured by Wikipedia's deletion logs.

By December 2025, the US Federal Trade Commission will fine a major AI company for deceptive marketing, claiming that their 'AI-powered' tool was actually a mechanical turk operation employing humans behind the scenes.

By end of 2025, at least one major textbook publisher (e.g., Pearson, McGraw Hill, Cengage) will release a textbook that is entirely written and illustrated by AI, with no human author or illustrator credited.
In the next 12 months, a major film studio will use AI to generate the entire soundtrack for a theatrical release, and the film will be nominated for an Oscar in a sound-related category.
In the next 12 months, the CEO of a major AI company will publicly state that AGI is not achievable within the next 10 years, contradicting previous more optimistic timelines.
A well-known AI image generator will be updated to refuse all requests involving dogs, due to an internal policy misinterpretation, sparking a public backlash and a quick reversal.

By October 2025, the pandas library will add an AI-powered PR reviewer that rejects all contributions containing explicit for loops in favor of vectorized operations, sparking a community fork with over 1000 stars within 3 months.

By August 2025, a U.S. federal financial regulator (SEC or CFPB) will officially approve a linear regression model for mortgage eligibility decisions, explicitly citing its interpretability after a publicized failure of a deep learning model in lending.
Within 12 months, Apple will launch a subscription for 'AI compute credits' bundled with iCloud+ rather than a standalone LLM service.

By next year, a major LLM provider will release a dedicated 'long-context' model with >1M token window, priced per-token at a premium, and marketed specifically for codebase analysis rather than conversation.
Within 12 months, a major US airline will publicly deploy an AI-based dynamic pricing system for checked baggage fees, based on real-time demand and flight load factors, rather than fixed pricing.

Within 12 months, a major cloud provider (AWS, Azure, or GCP) will launch a marketplace allowing individuals to rent out their personal GPUs for AI training, using sandboxed execution and blockchain-backed escrow.

By next year, a major smartphone manufacturer (Samsung or Google) will ship a phone with a dedicated, physically separate AI coprocessor advertised for on-device privacy, rather than integrating it into the main SoC.

Within 12 months, a major US airline will implement AI-based dynamic pricing for checked baggage fees, but will abandon it within 3 months due to customer confusion and regulatory pressure, then revert to fixed pricing.

Within 12 months, a major e-commerce platform (e.g., eBay, Etsy) will introduce an AI system that automatically generates 'suggested price reductions' for stale listings (unsold for >30 days) based on demand signals, but will default to an opt-out model where sellers must manually disable the feature per listing, leading to silent price drops for millions of items.

Within 12 months, a major US telecom (e.g., T-Mobile, Verizon) will introduce a 'spectrum-sensing' AI feature on 5G towers that dynamically reallocates unused frequency bands to local businesses for private network use, sold as a per-minute add-on to existing plans.

By next year, a popular open-source hardware project (e.g., Raspberry Pi) will release a dedicated 'AI inference hat' that costs under $30 and is specifically marketed for running local LLMs on the Pi 5, but will be limited to 128MB of on-board memory for the model, forcing extreme model quantization.

Within 12 months, a prominent mid-range PC manufacturer (e.g., Dell, HP) will ship a laptop with a 'neural processing unit' (NPU) dedicated only to background noise removal in video calls, but will not advertise it as AI—just as 'wind cancelation'—and will disable the NPU for any other use via driver lock.

Within 12 months, a major online dating app (e.g., Tinder, Bumble) will introduce an AI 'profile trust score' derived from photo metadata and text analysis, but will only show it to paying subscribers, not to free users.
